paper.title: RLP: Reinforcement as a Pretraining Objective

problem_statement: The standard training pipeline for large reasoning models uses next-token prediction (NTP) during pretraining, followed by supervised fine-tuning, and finally reinforcement learning (RL) as the last phase. RL from human/verifiable feedback is effective for reasoning, but requires task-specific checkers or curated solutions to compute rewards — limiting its applicability to specific domains. Pretraining with NTP alone does not explicitly reward reasoning or integrate world knowledge during learning; it only maximizes likelihood of observed tokens. The question: Can we bring the core benefit of RL — incentivizing useful thinking — into pretraining itself, applied to general web-scale text without any task-specific verifiers?
known_ingredients: Next-token prediction (NTP) maximizes the log-likelihood of the next token given a context, with no mechanism to reward or incentivize internal reasoning before predicting

Chain-of-thought (CoT) prompting demonstrates that explicit reasoning steps before answering dramatically improves performance on complex tasks — but CoT is only useful if the model generates actually helpful reasoning, not just plausible-sounding filler

RLHF and RLVR work by computing reward signals from external verifiers (human preferences, correctness checkers), but these require task-specific setup for each domain and cannot be applied to generic pretraining text

Information gain (mutual information) is a task-agnostic measure of how useful one variable is for predicting another: IG(CoT → next token) = log P(next_token | context, CoT) - log P(next_token | context alone)

EMA (exponential moving average) of model parameters provides a slowly-changing 'lagged' baseline version of the model — widely used in self-supervised learning (e.g., BYOL, MoCo) to provide a stable reference without a separate model

GRPO (Group Relative Policy Optimization) is a variant of PPO that uses group-sampled rewards for variance reduction without requiring a learned value function — used in recent reasoning model training
Reward hacking in RL occurs when the agent finds ways to maximize the reward signal without actually improving the target behavior — a known risk when the reward is computed from the model itself
key_insight: Use information gain as a verifier-free, dense reward signal during pretraining: treat generating a chain-of-thought before predicting each next token as an RL action, and reward it based on how much the CoT improves the log-likelihood of the actual observed next token compared to a no-think baseline. The no-think baseline is provided by an EMA teacher (a slowly-updated copy of the model), which prevents reward hacking. This reward is available at every token position in ordinary web text — no task-specific verifier needed. The thought policy and predictor share the same model weights; gradients are applied only to the thought tokens using a GRPO-style clipped surrogate. This brings RL-style exploration and reasoning incentivization into pretraining on arbitrary text, earlier and more efficiently than post-training RL.
insight_components: Information gain (log P(next_token | context, CoT) − log P(next_token | context)) is a self-contained, verifier-free reward that measures whether a CoT is genuinely useful for predicting the next token — not just coherent-sounding

An EMA lagged teacher provides the 'no-think' baseline counterfactual, avoiding the degenerate case where the model compares to itself (which collapses the reward to zero) while remaining informative enough to detect improvement
The reward signal applies to every token in ordinary pretraining text — no special data curation or task-specific verifiers — making it as broadly applicable as NTP itself
Gradients are applied only to the thought tokens (not the predicted token or baseline), using a GRPO-style clipped surrogate for stability; the predictor and thought policy share weights
difficulty_rationale: The ingredients give the right building blocks (NTP, CoT, IG, EMA, GRPO) but the specific combination is non-obvious. The key jumps are: (1) using log-likelihood ratio as the reward rather than external verification — most would default to verifiers; (2) using an EMA teacher as the no-think baseline to prevent reward hacking — without this the system degenerates; (3) applying gradients only to thought tokens, not predictions — the training target distinction is subtle. All three must be combined correctly.

contamination_probe_results.q1_title_recognition: Not recognized — paper published September 2025, after model training cutoff.
contamination_probe_results.q2_proposed_approach_summary: Probe proposed outcome reward models, self-supervised auxiliary objectives, process supervision with general signals, and preference learning on pretraining data. Did NOT propose the actual insight: information gain (log P(next_token | context, CoT) − log P(next_token | context)) as a verifier-free dense reward, using an EMA teacher for the no-think baseline, applying GRPO only to thought tokens.
contamination_probe_results.human_tier_rationale: Paper published September 2025, definitively post-cutoff. Probe proposes completely different approaches — the specific EMA-teacher information-gain reward with GRPO on thought tokens is not guessable. Clean benchmark case.
